Matson Studios · Exhibition mocks · Editions 01–05 + The Season

The Hang

Exhibition walkthroughs built from the archive: which frames earn wall space, what they're printed on, and exactly how they hang — wall by wall, to scale. Five editions: the flagship two-room survey, the all-spider show, the reptile house, the 35mm diary, and a motor-event pop-up. The Season, at the bottom, is the census that maps them.

Edition 01 — “Field Work”

Flagship · two rooms · 16 works

The archive splits cleanly into two bodies that flatter each other: digital macro — sharp, intimate, scientifically literate — and the 35mm diary — loose, warm, grain-forward. Fighting that split would water both down; the show leans into it instead. Room A, “Undergrowth”, is painted charcoal and lit with tight spots so the frameless prints float like specimens in a vitrine. Room B, “Roads South”, is standard gallery white with oak-framed C-types at postcard-modest sizes. You walk from the world at 2:1 magnification into the world at 1:50,000 — same photographer, same patience, different lens.

Room A — Undergrowth

Charcoal walls · frameless dibond · 3000K spots

FIELD WORK

Photographs · Steven Matson

01 · THE KING · 100 × 150 · DIBOND
WALL 1 / 6

The greeting

Hero · hung alone

One work, two metres of breathing room either side, title vinyl to its left. The cobra's eye contact does the job a curator's introduction normally does. Centre of the image at 152 cm — it meets every visitor at eye level.

02 · SPINE · 60 × 90
03 · DEPTH STUDY · 60 × 76
04 · PLANE · 90 × 60
05 · STACK · 60 × 90
WALL 2 / 6

The watchers

Spider line · common centreline

Four spiders, one straight line, mixed orientations sharing a 152 cm centre. The rhythm portrait–portrait–landscape–portrait stops the wall feeling like a contact sheet. Labels carry species names — the science is part of the work.

06 · VIOLA · 40 × 60
07 · DIANTHUS · 40 × 60
08 · FOCUS · 60 × 90
WALL 3 / 6

The dark garden

Botanical pair + chameleon

The flowers and the chameleon share the same trick — colour burning out of a black ground — so they close Room A as one family. Tight 24 cm gaps pull the trio together; the chameleon's larger size keeps hierarchy.

Room B — Roads South

Gallery white · oak frames, white mats · C-type prints

09 · INTERFERENCE · 50 × 75
10 · GLOOM · 50 × 75
11 · DUSK · 50 × 75
12 · VILLA · 50 × 75
13 · CHAPEL · 50 × 75
WALL 4 / 6

The diary line

Five C-types · identical framing

Five frames, one size, one frame spec, equal 24 cm gaps — a metronome. Sequenced cold to warm: Copenhagen's interference into Iberian dusk. Identical presentation makes the colour shift the entire story.

14 · CAVALLINO · 40 × 62
15 · SILBERPFEIL · 52 × 58
WALL 5 / 6

The paddock pair

Two works · short wall

Red and silver, Italy and Germany, hung as a conversation on the short wall. Car photography can drift towards poster culture — small sizes and serious framing keep these as photographs first.

16 · FOUR BOATS · 60 × 80 · FINAL WALL
WALL 6 / 6

The exhale

Closer · hung alone

Every show needs a last image people stand in front of before they leave. Four Boats is the quietest thing you've made — after the cobra's stare and five countries of film, it lets the room go silent. Visitors exit past the price list.

The run-through

If you actually book the room

01 · PRINTING

Match medium to source

  • Digital macro (R7, 32 MP): dibond or acrylic, full bleed, matte laminate. Safe to 100 × 150; the 40-frame stack will hold detail at any size you can afford.
  • 35mm scans: cap at 50 × 75. Grain is the aesthetic, mush isn't. C-type on a matte stock, never gloss.
  • Order one proof print of the cobra and one film frame before committing the run — check blacks on dibond and the red channel on Portra scans.
02 · FRAMING

Two specs, no exceptions

  • Room A: frameless dibond with hidden subframe — the image floats 2 cm off the wall.
  • Room B: 25 mm natural oak, white 7 cm mat, art glass (anti-reflective) on everything under spots.
  • Uniform specs are what make 16 works read as one show — resist a special frame for a favourite.
03 · HANGING

152 cm and a laser level

  • Centre of every image at 152 cm. Groups share the centreline, not top edges.
  • Gaps: 24 cm inside a group, 1.5–2 m around heroes (walls 1 and 6).
  • Hang order: hero first, then walls 2–3, then the diary line — if space fights back, the pairs flex, the heroes don't.
04 · LIGHTING

Room A dark, Room B bright

  • Room A: walls in charcoal (F&B Off-Black or equivalent), tight 3000 K spots at 30°, one per work. Ambient low.
  • Room B: even wash, same 3000 K — don't mix colour temperatures mid-show.
  • Dibond hates glare: aim spots so reflections fall below eye line.
05 · LABELS & EDITIONS

The science is the label

  • Labels: title, species in italics, capture data (lens, aperture, stack count), stock for film works, price. Your BRAND.md voice, verbatim.
  • One QR per room to the portfolio site, not one per work.
  • Editions as per hang list; sign and number verso, certificate for the hero.
06 · MONEY & OPENING

Rough budget

  • Printing + framing all 16: expect £1,800–2,400 at UK lab rates; the oak frames are half of it.
  • Full sell-through at guide prices ≈ £3,940 on first editions alone — the show pays for itself at ~60% sold.
  • Opening night: species-ID cards as flyers, the Print Room mockups on a screen, postcards (chapel + palms) at the till for £3 — the low-commitment purchase every visitor makes.

Edition 02 — “Eight Eyes”

All-spider show · one dark room · 9 works

The spider work is the deepest seam in the archive — twelve strong frames and counting — and it deserves a show that doesn't share billing. One room, near-black walls, tight pools of light: the gallery becomes the underside of a leaf. Where Edition 01 impresses, Edition 02 converts — people arrive wary of spiders and leave having stood nose-to-nose with one for ten minutes.

01 · THE AMBUSH · 120 × 80 · DIBOND
E02 · WALL 1 / 4

The ambush

Hero · hung alone

Four gold-rimmed eyes emerging from darkness — on a near-black wall the print has no visible edge, so the eyes simply hang in the room. The single strongest opener in the whole archive; it sets the show's rule that the dark is full of watchers.

02 · WATCHER I · 75 × 50
03 · WATCHER II · 75 × 60
04 · WATCHER III · 75 × 50
E02 · WALL 2 / 4

The watchers

Portrait row · landscape crops

Three salticid portraits at increasing magnification — approach, meet, and closer-than-comfortable. Cropped landscape and hung as one line so the eyes track you down the wall. Species and stack data on the labels do the educating.

05 · THE MEAL · 60 × 90
06 · THE TRAP · 90 × 60
07 · THE ENGINEER · 60 × 90
08 · THE LINE · 60 × 90
E02 · WALL 3 / 4

Fieldcraft

Behaviour wall · four works

The show's argument: spiders as workers, not monsters. Hunting, trapping, engineering, abseiling — each label describes the behaviour in your census-taker voice. This is the wall where the ecology degree shows.

09 · THE SHY ONE · 60 × 94 · FINAL WALL
E02 · WALL 4 / 4

The shy one

Closer · hung alone

After an hour of being watched, the last spider hides from you. It reverses the show's power dynamic in one frame and sends people out smiling — the same role Four Boats plays in Edition 01, done with eight legs.

Edition 03 — “Vivarium”

Life behind glass · one dark room · 6 works

The reptile-house portraits treat captive animals with the same seriousness as the hedgerow spiders — eye contact, patience, no glass reflections, no crowds. One dark room again, but warmer light than Eight Eyes: vivarium light. The film pair at the end (a sloth, a butterfly) breaks the digital run on purpose — glass houses shot on grain feel like memory.

VIVARIUM

Life behind glass · Steven Matson

01 · THE STONE THAT BREATHES · 80 × 120 · DIBOND
E03 · WALL 1 / 3

The stone that breathes

Hero · hung alone

The Mangshan viper on grey rock is the show's thesis in one frame — you look at stone for three seconds before you find the eye. Camouflage does the curating: people will stand here longest of any wall in any edition.

02 · THE KING · 60 × 90
03 · THE DESCENT · 60 × 90
04 · THE RELIC · 60 × 90
E03 · WALL 2 / 3

Three keepers

Portrait trio · equal sizes

Cobra, chameleon, crocodile lizard — three heads, three ancient lineages, one size and one centreline so no species outranks another. Labels carry conservation status alongside the usual capture data; the crocodile lizard's endangered listing earns its place in the middle.

05 · AT REST · 52 × 36 · C-TYPE, OAK
06 · PERCH · 40 × 60 · C-TYPE, OAK
E03 · WALL 3 / 3

The soft room

Film pair · the exhale

After three predators, a sloth asleep in a basket and a paper kite butterfly — both shot on 35mm inside glass houses. The grain and the oak frames slow the show right down; this wall is where the gift-shop postcards come from.

Edition 04 — “Roads South”

The 35mm diary · gallery white · 11 works, one day's light

The film archive hung as a single day: morning sun, golden hour, nightfall. Everything C-type in oak, everything modest in size — this is the show for a café-gallery or festival fringe, priced so people actually take one home. It opens with a dead vending machine and closes with four boats going nowhere; the diary's whole voice is in that pair.

ROADS SOUTH

A 35mm diary · 2022–2024

01 · RECLAIMED · 60 × 88 · HERO
E04 · WALL 1 / 5

The opener

Hero · hung alone

A vending machine being eaten by a hedge is the perfect first page for a travel diary — commerce loses, plants win, and the sun flares right on cue. It tells visitors this is a show about noticing, not about destinations.

02 · GARDA VIEWS · 50 × 75
03 · VILLA HIBISCUS · 50 × 75
04 · SUNLIT CHAPEL · 50 × 75
E04 · WALL 2 / 5

Morning

Full sun · blue-sky trio

Hard Mediterranean light, saturated Kodak blues, subjects standing to attention. Hung in reading order the day begins here — the wall label is just a time: “09:00–14:00”.

05 · PALM TREES · 50 × 75
06 · HALF TURN · 50 × 75
07 · PORTUGUESE HORIZON · 50 × 75
E04 · WALL 3 / 5

Golden hour

The warm wall

Palms, the fairground wheel mid-rotation, the villa catching its last direct light. Same sizes, same frames — the palette shift from noon blue to honey is the only event, and it reads left to right like a sunset.

08 · BLUE PLAINS · 66 × 50
09 · DUSK · 50 × 75
10 · INTERIOR, LAST LIGHT · 50 × 75
E04 · WALL 4 / 5

Nightfall

Dusk triptych

Mountains going flat, the motorway lighting up, and finally an olive sprig indoors with the day's last blade of sun — the diary comes home and shuts the door. Landscape–portrait–portrait keeps the wall from going static.

11 · FOUR BOATS · 60 × 80 · FINAL WALL
E04 · WALL 5 / 5

The morning after

Closer · hung alone

Four Boats works even harder here than in Field Work — after a whole day of roads, nothing moves. (It appears in both editions because they'd never hang at once; whichever show books first gets it.)

Edition 05 — “Paddock”

Pop-up · scaffold & ply wall · 6 works · posters, not prints

Not a white cube — a pop-up for a motor event, paddock club night or festival stand. Prints pinned to a ply hoarding like period race posters, sold as open-edition A2s off a rack beside the wall. Different audience, different economics: this show is merchandise with a hero wall, and it can be up in an afternoon.

01 · ROSSO · 80 × 120 · ACRYLIC — THE ONE SERIOUS PRINT
E05 · WALL 1 / 3

Rosso

Hero · acrylic face-mount

The near-abstract red bonnet is the only work that gets gallery treatment — a glossy acrylic mount so the paint reads as paint. Everything else in the room is deliberately rough; one immaculate object makes the scaffold look intentional.

02 · CAVALLINO · A2 POSTER
03 · MIURA · A2 POSTER
04 · SILBERPFEIL · A2 POSTER
E05 · WALL 2 / 3

The legends

Poster row · pinned

P4, Miura, C9 — pinned slightly off-true like a race-shop wall. These are the three frames people will buy on the spot; the rack beside the wall holds the open editions at £25.

05 · NIGHT STAGE · A2 POSTER
06 · SERVICE PARK, 22:40 · A2 POSTER
E05 · WALL 3 / 3

After dark

The rally pair

The night-film pair closes the pop-up where the sport actually lives — a cold service park at 22:40. Grain, floodlights, red plastic chairs. If the Jersey Rally will host the stand, this wall is the pitch.

The Season — how many shows are in the archive?

Census of ~46 frames · grouped by theme · readiness rated

Full audit of the portfolio: two shows are hangable today, one is a print-order away, and three more are each one good shoot from opening. That's a two-year exhibition programme already sitting on your hard drive. Readiness counts only frames strong enough to print large.

SHOW 1 · READY — MOCKED ABOVE

Field Work

16 works · flagship. The two-room macro + film survey, fully specced above. The one to pitch to a proper gallery first — it shows range.

SHOW 2 · READY — MOCKED ABOVE

Eight Eyes

9–12 works · one dark room. The all-spider show. Deepest bench in the archive (The Ambush, the Watcher portraits, the behaviour set). Perfect for a science-festival or museum slot, where the species labels become the draw.

SHOW 3 · MOCKED ABOVE — A PRINT ORDER AWAY

Roads South

~14 works · all 35mm. Dusk, Villa, Chapel, Palms, Blue Plains, Half Turn (the ferris wheel), Reclaimed (the overgrown Coke machine), Hibiscus, Garda, Boats… A warm film-diary show — café-gallery or photography-festival fringe, framed small and priced to sell.

SHOW 4 · MOCKED ABOVE — ONE ZOO VISIT FROM FULL SIZE

Vivarium

6 works now, needs ~10. Cobra, Mangshan viper, chameleon, crocodile lizard, croc skink, sloth. The reptile-house portraits are already museum-grade — one more session of glass-front portraits finishes a genuinely unusual show.

SHOW 5 · ONE SPRING AWAY

Hortus

7 works now, needs ~12. Buttercup, viola, both dianthus, white geranium, alkanet, the butterfly. The lit-flower-on-black formula is repeatable on demand — one spring of shooting completes a set that sells as prints better than anything else here.

SHOW 6 · MOCKED ABOVE — POP-UP READY

Paddock

7 works. Ferrari badge ×2, Sauber, both Miuras, Escort at night, Fabia service park. Not a white-cube show — a pop-up at a motor event or club night, hung on scaffold, sold as A2 posters. Different audience, fastest money.

Draft statement

Wall vinyl · Room A entrance · your voice, not art-speak

Most of these photographs were taken within a few miles of home. The spiders live in the hedge; the snakes and chameleons live behind glass; the roads are the ones we drove. Everything is exactly as found — the only trick is patience, and getting low enough to meet things at their own eye level.

— STEVEN MATSON · JERSEY, CHANNEL ISLANDS · FIELD WORK, 2026

All photographs © Steven Matson. Venue, prices and editions are working proposals — the frames, sizes and hang logic are ready to use. Companion piece: The Print Room (mockup series 01–03).
